title: Definable Norms on Riemann-Zariski Spaces
speaker: Michal Szachniewicz
abstract: I will talk about a joint work with Antoine Sedillot. We study sup-norms of sections of metrized line bundles for families of arithmetic varieties. Using Riemann-Zariski spaces we obtain formulas that imply new definability results in the context of globally valued fields. I will outline our setup and motivations.
tags: Workshop on Recent Developments in Hodge Theory and O-minimality

proof_stages.label: Diophantine and definability motivation
proof_stages.summary: The talk motivates a notion of definability different from o-minimality by contrasting the o-minimal route to Manin-Mumford-type results with the Arakelov/equidistribution route to Bogomolov-type small-point statements.

proof_stages.label: Small-point equidistribution as the target theorem
proof_stages.summary: Generic sequences whose heights attain the arithmetic minimum are recalled to equidistribute to the Chern/Haar measure, and this explains why arithmetic intersection numbers and Siu inequalities are the engine behind Bogomolov-type conclusions.

proof_stages.label: Arithmetic Siu inequality and arithmetic volumes
proof_stages.summary: The geometric Siu inequality is upgraded to an arithmetic inequality by defining arithmetic volume through small sections, sup norms, and covolumes of lattices, yielding Yuan's arithmetic Siu inequality for ample semipositive Hermitian line bundles.

proof_stages.label: Definable right-hand side via resultants
proof_stages.summary: The arithmetic intersection side is expressed as a limit of heights of Chow/resultant coefficients after Veronese embeddings, so it is definable from global height data on the ground field.

proof_stages.label: Global heights, places, and local sup norms
proof_stages.summary: Axiomatic global heights are represented by measures on spaces of absolute values, and projective embeddings induce placewise value and sup norms whose determinant norms define the degree/covolume expression needed for the left-hand side.

proof_stages.label: Riemann-Zariski model formalism and definability of the left-hand side
proof_stages.summary: Replacing the field and variety by inverse limits of projective models produces Riemann-Zariski spaces on which embeddings give line bundles, and pushforward/vector-bundle formulas show that the relevant volumes and norm data can be presented by height-definable formulas, with uniform arithmetic Siu remaining the hoped-for next step.

examples.name: Manin-Mumford torsion points on abelian varieties
examples.note: The torsion-point statement for subvarieties of abelian varieties illustrates the kind of Diophantine result whose o-minimal and distributional proofs the speaker is comparing.
examples.engagement_reason: The Manin-Mumford torsion-point statement is recalled as motivation and compared with Bogomolov, but no concrete torsion-point example is computed or checked.

passes.reason: Torsion points are the height-zero/minimal points for the Néron-Tate height and are used as motivating special points, not to refute a claim.
Torsion points are the finite-order, zero Néron–Tate-height boundary points used as motivation, not to refute a claim.

examples.name: Curve case of the resultant
examples.note: For a curve in projective space, the speaker notes that two hypersurfaces usually have no common intersection with it, and the resultant is the single polynomial detecting when they do.
examples.engagement_reason: The curve case is used only to illustrate the resultant’s vanishing condition for a curve and two hypersurfaces, without computing a resultant or carrying out the construction on a concrete curve.

passes.reason: The curve case is a standard low-dimensional illustration of the resultant, not a collapsed or adversarial example.
The curve resultant is an ordinary low-dimensional instance of the general resultant/Chow-form construction.
the curve resultant is the ordinary d=1 Chow-form/resultant instance used to explain the construction, not a pathological or adversarial case
the curve resultant is a standard low-dimensional instance of the general Chow/resultant construction, used illustratively
The curve case is just an illustrative low-dimensional instance of the general resultant/Chow-form construction.
The curve resultant is a standard low-dimensional illustration of the general resultant construction, not a sporadic, limiting, or adversarial case.
Used only to illustrate the resultant in the curve setting, which is an ordinary low-dimensional case rather than a refuting or structurally special object.

examples.name: Function-field height from a curve
examples.note: For the algebraic closure of k(t), the height is described by summing pole orders over closed points of a normalizing curve, making the global-height data concrete outside the number-field case.
examples.engagement_reason: The function-field height is explicitly constructed from a curve by summing minus the minimum valuations of the functions at closed points, weighted by degrees.

passes.reason: The curve-defined function-field height is a standard construction of a global height and is not deployed as a counterexample.
The height defined by summing curve valuations in a function field is a standard systematic example of a global height.
the valuation-degree height attached to a curve is a standard function-field global height used as another model example
